revision.comment: 外部リンクの修正 http:// -> https:// (www.nikkei.com) ([[Wikipedia:Bot|Bot]]による編集)
revision.text:
{{別人|x1=「森浩一」の登録名を使用していたプロ野球選手の|森一晃}} {{Infobox 学者 | name = 森 浩一 | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[大阪府]][[大阪市]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | death_place = {{JPN}} [[京都府]][[京都市]] | othername = |main_interests = 日本考古学 |alma_mater = <!--母校--> |workplaces = [[同志社大学]] |degree = <!--学位--> | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }} '''森 浩一'''（もり こういち、[[1928年]][[7月17日]] - [[2013年]][[8月6日]]<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref>）は、[[日本]]の[[考古学者]]。[[同志社大学]]名誉教授。専門は、日本考古学・日本文化史学。
== 略歴 == [[大阪府]][[大阪市]]出身。少年時代は[[堺市]]近郊に住み、[[尋常小学校]]の国史の[[国定教科書]]における[[日本神話|記紀神話]]と、堺市の[[百舌鳥古墳群]]を通じて、[[古墳]]及び[[陵墓]]への関心を抱く。大阪府立堺中学校（現・[[大阪府立三国丘高等学校]]）卒業。1951年に[[同志社大学文学部]]英文学科卒業<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref>、1957年、同[[大学院]]文学研究科文化史学専攻[[大学院#修士課程・博士前期課程|修士課程]]修了。学生時代から古墳の発掘と報告書作成に取り組む（例：[[黄金塚古墳]]）。学生考古学研究会も創設（後に[[古代学研究会]]）。
卒業後は[[大阪府立泉大津高等学校|府立泉大津高校]]の教諭を務める<ref name="kouyuukai"/>傍ら、[[新沢千塚古墳群|新沢千塚]]（[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]）など古墳の発掘に従事。その後東京大学の[[井上光貞]]により『日本の歴史』（[[中央公論社]]）の考古学担当の執筆者に抜擢される（日本の歴史1神話から歴史へ 「40年のちのあとがき」より）<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20120313184525/http://www.asahi.com/culture/news_culture/TKY201202020353.html〈時の回廊〉森浩一「古墳の発掘」 天皇陵の疑念 世に問う] 朝日新聞 2012年2月3日配信</ref>。[[関西大学]]講師を経て1965年、急逝した[[酒詰仲男]]（東大卒人類学専攻）の後任として同志社大学専任講師となり、1972年には同志社大学文学部教授。同志社の顔とも呼ばれる名物教授だった。1999年に退任。
2010年には[[愛知県]][[春日井市]]に蔵書を寄贈し、[[春日井市立中央公民館]]に「[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]」が設立された<ref>[https://archive.ph/yVLib 森浩一さん死去:考古学、分かりやすく 権威主義に反発] 毎日 2013-8-10配信</ref>。2012年3月、永年の考古学・古代史への貢献により第22回[[南方熊楠賞]]を受賞する<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[朝日新聞]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref><ref name=三丘同窓会>{{Cite web|和書|url= http://sankyuu.sakura.ne.jp/topics/2012topics/chu47mori.html |title=中47期・森浩一氏に南方賞|publisher=三丘同窓会|accessdate=2020-7-26}}</ref>。2013年8月6日午後8時54分、急性心不全のため京都市内の病院で死去した。{{没年齢|1928|7|17|2013|8|6}}<ref name="nikkei20130809"/><ref name="sankei20130810">「古墳研究の第一人者 森浩一氏 死去」『産経新聞』 2013年8月10日付け、東京本社発行15版、23面。</ref>。
== 研究内容・業績 == ===考古学者として=== *[[考古学]]研究の第一人者として長く活動している。若い頃から当時の考古学界をリードする[[梅原末治]]、[[末永雅雄]]、[[八幡一郎]]、[[水野清一]]、[[江上波夫]]などと交流があり、独自の観点から研究発表を行った。同志社の学生時代には雑誌『古代学研究』を発刊し、民間の研究者を含め多くの研究者に発表の場を提供した。同誌の100号発刊時には、江上波夫、[[有光教一]]、[[岸俊男]]などそうそうたるメンバーが発起人となり祝賀会を開催している。 *著作には『渡来人』、『検証 古代日本と[[百済]]』、『巨大古墳』、『僕が歩いた古代史への道』、『日本神話の考古学』、『記紀の考古学』など多数がある。一般書だけでも100冊を超える<ref name="朝日"/>。 *早くから「[[三角縁神獣鏡]]国産説」を打ち出した<ref name=朝日/><ref name=三丘同窓会/><ref name="sankei20130810" />。 *近著では従来の考古学のアプローチとは異なる、地域に対する多面的視点から多くの問題を提起している。古代史に関しては、井上光貞（東大）や岸俊男（京大）など、当時の歴史学会を代表する学者と親しい。
=== テーマ別業績 === # 中央（畿内）史観にとらわれない発想（[[三角縁神獣鏡]]配布論批判や前方後円墳の波及をヤマト王権の確立と連動させない） # 遺物ではなく遺跡を中心に考える（故京大名誉教授水野清一の考えを受け継ぐ） # 天皇陵（証明出来ない）を在地の名前で呼ぶ # 遺跡の保存運動（イタスケ古墳） # 地域の重視（地域学のすすめ）
=== 天皇陵に指定されている古墳に関する見解 === *天皇陵（[[古墳]]）についての活発な問題提起をしている<ref name="nikkei20130809" />。例えば、[[仁徳陵]]を大仙古墳と呼ぶべしなど、証明の出来無い天皇陵を所在地名で呼ぶことを提唱<ref name=朝日/>。{{要出典範囲|date=2021年7月23日 (金) 01:44 (UTC)|現在では、多くの社会科の教科書で実現}}。 * 1965年に[[見瀬丸山古墳]][[欽明天皇]]陵比定説を発表（提唱）した研究者でもあり、現在、この説は有力説となっている<!-- ＊ 「季刊『考古学』 別冊2 『見瀬丸山古墳と天皇陵』 猪熊兼勝編 1992年」 -->。
===交遊=== *研究者だけでなく、作家の[[松本清張]]・[[司馬遼太郎]]・[[黒岩重吾]]とも交流があった。司馬遼太郎とは数回「街道を行く」の取材に同行している。また松本清張の考古学に関する知識のアドバイザーでもあった<ref name="三丘同窓会"/>。
==家族・親族== *従姉妹：[[須賀敦子]]は[[イタリア文学]]者で随筆家。<ref>{{Cite|和書|author=森浩一|title=森浩一の考古交友録|chapter=須賀敦子さん 幼時遊んだ従姉妹はすれ違いの仲|publisher=朝日新聞社|pages=64-67|ncid=BB12498114|year=2013|ref=harv}}</ref>。
== 著述物一覧 == === 著作 === * 古墳の発掘 (中央公論社 [[中公新書]] 1965.4) * 古墳―石と土の造形 ([[保育社]][[カラーブックス]](212) 1970.1、単行新版1973) * [[古墳時代]]の考古学―シンポジウム([[学生社|學生社]] 1970) * 黄金塚古墳 (美術文化シリーズ) ([[中央公論美術出版]] 1971) * [[井辺八幡山古墳]] ([[和歌山市]][[教育委員会]] 1972) * 古墳文化小考([[三省堂]]新書 1974/[[社会思想社]] [[現代教養文庫]] 1979) * 古墳と古代文化99の謎 ([[産報]]サンポウ・ブックス 1976) * [[考古学]]入門 ([[保育社]]カラーブックス 1976) * 古代史の宝庫 ([[朝日新聞社]] 1977.12) * 考古学の摸索 ([[學生社]] 1978.3) * 考古学 西から東から（中公文庫 1984.6） ;※この間も多数刊行 * 古代史津々浦々(1993年2月、[[小学館]]/1997年5月、[[小学館]]ライブラリー） * 考古学―その見方と解釈〈上・下〉(1991年6月-1993年4月、[[筑摩書房]]) * 日本神話の考古学(1993年7月、朝日新聞社/1999年2月、[[朝日文庫]]) * 森浩一の語る 日本の古代 (1)(1994年2月、[[大巧社]]) * 考古紀行[[騎馬民族]]の道はるか―[[高句麗]]古墳がいま語るもの(1994年3月、[[日本放送出版協会]]) * 考古学と古代日本(1994年3月、中央公論社) * [[平安京]]の不思議―古都に眠る歴史の謎を訪ねて(1994年3月、[[PHP研究所]]) * 古代史の窓(1995年9月、[[新潮社]]/1998年8月、[[新潮文庫]]) * 食の体験文化史 (1995年9月、中央公論社/1999年10月、[[中公文庫]]) * [[天皇陵]][[古墳]](1996年1月、大巧社) * 食の体験文化史〈2〉(1997年4月、中央公論社／「続・食の体験文化史」中公文庫、2000年9月) * 考古学へのまなざし―地中から甦る本当の歴史(1998年6月、大巧社) * 僕は考古学に鍛えられた (1998年12月、[[筑摩書房]]／2012年1月、[[ちくま文庫]]) * 食の体験文化史〈3〉(1999年1月、中央公論社) * [[考古学]]入門(2000年、[[保育社]][[カラーブックス]]) * 巨大古墳―治水王と[[天皇陵]] (2000年8月、[[講談社学術文庫]]) * 僕が歩いた古代史への道 (2000年10月、[[角川文庫]]) * 記紀の考古学(2000年11月、[[朝日新聞社]]) 。各・再刊、2005年5月、朝日文庫。2024年3月、[[角川新書]] * 関東学をひらく―調査ノート1999‐2000(2001年3月、朝日新聞社) * 語っておきたい古代史―[[倭人]]・[[クマソ]]・[[天皇]]をめぐって (2001年5月、[[新潮文庫]]) * 地域学のすすめ―考古学からの提言 (2002年7月、[[岩波書店]] [[岩波新書]]) * 僕と歩こう全国50遺跡 考古学の旅 (2002年8月、[[小学館]]) * わが青春の考古学 (2002年9月、[[新潮文庫]]) * 僕の古代史発掘（2003年2月、[[角川選書]]） * 魂の考古学―時のすぎゆくままに(2003年10月、五月書房) * 山野河海の列島史（2004年2月、[[朝日選書]]） * 海から知る考古学入門―古代人との対話(2004年12月、[[角川書店]] [[角川oneテーマ21]]) * ぼくの考古古代学(2005年3月、[[日本放送出版協会]]) * 「東海学」事始め―[[東海]]の歴史を歩く(2005年7月、學生社) * 回想の食卓 (2006年9月、大巧社) * 京都の歴史を足元からさぐる [[洛東]]の巻 (2007年7月、学生社) * 古代史おさらい帖―考古学・古代学課題ノート (2007年10月、[[筑摩書房]]/2011年10月、ちくま学芸文庫) * 京都の歴史を足元からさぐる [[洛北]]・[[上京区|上京]]・[[山科]]の巻 (2008年03月、学生社) * 京都の歴史を足元からさぐる [[北野 (京都市)|北野]]・紫野・洛中の巻 (2008年10月、学生社) * 森浩一、食った記録（2005年、[[編集グループSURE]]） * 日本の深層文化（2009年7月、ちくま新書） * [[魏志倭人伝|倭人伝]]を読みなおす、(2010年8月、[[ちくま新書]]) * [[萬葉集]]に歴史を読む（2011年2月、ちくま学芸文庫） * [[天皇陵]]古墳への招待（2011年8月、[[筑摩選書]]） * 森浩一の考古交友録（2013年4月、[[朝日新聞出版]]） * 敗者の古代史（2013年6月、[[中経出版]]。2016年10月、中経の文庫。2022年9月、角川新書
;共著ほか * 考古学からみた古代の韓国と日本 (1993年3月、[[學生社]]) * 南海の王国[[琉球諸島|琉球]]の世紀―東アジアの中の[[琉球諸島|琉球]] (1993年4月、[[角川書店]]) * 稲と鉄 さまざまな王権の基盤 日本民俗文化大系 (1994年11月、[[小学館]]) * [[邪馬台国]]と吉野ケ里 (1997年7月、學生社) * 空からみた古墳 (2000年5月、[[学生社]]) * 日本史への挑戦―「関東学」の創造をめざして(共著；[[網野善彦]])(2000年8月、大巧社／2008年12月、[[ちくま学芸文庫]])
=== 対談 === * 対談古代文化の謎をめぐって(共著；[[上田正昭]], [[大林太良]])([[社会思想社]] 1977/01) * 馬・船・[[常民]](共著；[[網野善彦]])(1992年05月、[[河合出版]]) 1999年10月[[講談社学術文庫]] から再刊 * 対論 [[銅鐸]](共著；[[石野博信]])(1994年06月、[[學生社]]) * 森浩一対談集 古代技術の復権 ([[小学館]]ライブラリー)(1994年07月、[[小学館]]) * 古代史を解く『鍵』キーワード(共著；[[門脇禎二]])(1995年10月、[[學生社]]) * この国のすがたを歴史に読む(共著；[[網野善彦]])(2000年3月、[[大巧社]]) * 京都学ことはじめ―森浩一12のお勉強(2004年10月,[[編集グループSURE]]) * 対論 日本人の考古学(共著；[[佐原真]])(2004年10月、[[學生社]])
=== シンポジウム === * 古代日本[[金石文]]の謎 (エコール・ド・ロイヤル 古代日本を考える)(1991年11月、[[學生社]]) * 大王陵と古代豪族の謎(エコール・ド・ロイヤル 古代日本を考える)(1992年10月、[[學生社]]) * 日本像を問い直す―「海と列島文化」完結記念シンポジウム (1993年11月、[[小学館]]) * 継体大王と[[尾張国|尾張]]の目子媛―新王朝を支えた濃尾の豪族たち(1994年02月、[[小学館]]) * [[ヤマトタケル]]―尾張・[[美濃国|美濃]]と英雄伝説 第2回春日井シンポジウム(1995年12月、[[大巧社]]) * [[壬申の乱]]―大海人皇子から[[天武天皇]]へ―第3回春日井シンポジウム(1996年11月、大巧社) * [[飛騨国|飛騨]]―よみがえる山国の歴史―飛騨国府シンポジウム(1997年08月、大巧社) * 日本像を問い直す([[小学館]]ライブラリー)(1997年09月、[[小学館]]) * 瀬戸内の[[海人]]たち―'97しまなみシンポジウム(1997年11月、[[中国新聞社]]) * [[渡来人]]―尾張・美濃と渡来文化―第4回春日井シンポジウム(1997年12月、大巧社) * [[古墳時代]]の考古学 (シンポジウム 日本の考古学)(1998年03月、[[學生社]]) * [[縄文時代]]の考古学 (シンポジウム 日本の考古学)(1998年05月、[[學生社]]) * [[旧石器時代 (日本)|旧石器時代]]の考古学 (シンポジウム 日本の考古学)(1998年05月、[[學生社]]) * [[弥生時代]]の考古学 (シンポジウム 日本の考古学)(1998年09月、[[學生社]]) * 古代史のなかの女性たち―第5回春日井シンポジウム(1998年11月、大巧社) * 瀬戸内の[[海人]]たちII―'98しまなみシンポジウム(1998年12月、[[愛媛新聞]]) * 継体大王と[[渡来人]]―枚方歴史フォーラム(1998年12月、大巧社) * 旅の古代史―道・橋・関をめぐって 第6回春日井シンポジウム(1999年11月、大巧社) * 継体王朝―日本古代史の謎に挑む 第7回春日井シンポジウム(2000年12月、[[五月書房]]) * 東海学の創造をめざして―考古学と歴史学の諸問題 第8回春日井シンポジウム(2001年11月、五月書房) * 東海学が歴史を変える―弥生から[[伊勢平氏]]まで 第9回春日井シンポジウム(2002年11月、五月書房) * 検証・古代日本と[[百済]]―枚方歴史フォーラム(2003年4月、大巧社) * 東海学と日本文化―地域学をめざして 第10回春日井シンポジウム(2003年11月、五月書房) * 地域学から歴史を読む―第11回春日井シンポジウム(2004年12月、大巧社) * 水とまつりの古代史―第12回春日井シンポジウム(2005年12月、大巧社) * 伝説に歴史を読む―第13回春日井シンポジウム(2006年12月、大巧社)
=== 編著 === * 歪められた古代史([[井上清 (歴史家)|井上清]]と共編）([[毎日新聞社]] 1973) * 終末期古墳―論集 (塙書房 1973) * 馬 (日本古代文化の探究)([[社会思想社]] 1974) * 鉄 (日本古代文化の探究)(社会思想社 1974) * 墓地 (日本古代文化の探究)[社会思想社 1975) * 考古学ゼミナール(共編)([[山川出版社]] 1976) * 池 (日本古代文化の探究)(社会思想社 1978/2) * 鏡 (日本古代文化の探究)(社会思想社 1978/9) * 古代王権と玉の謎(1991年07月、[[新人物往来社]]) * 古代通信(1992年06月、[[學生社]]) * 海と列島文化(1)-(10)(総索引)(共編)(1990年07月-1993年02月、[[小学館]]) * 日本の古代遺跡(1)―(52)(1982?-1996年、[[保育社]]) * 日本の古代(1)―(15)(別巻) (共編)([[中公文庫]])(1995年01月-1997年02月、[[中央公論新社]]) * [[味噌]]・[[醤油]]・[[酒]]の来た道 (小学館ライブラリー)(1998年1月、小学館) * 古代探求―森浩一70の疑問(1998年07月、中央公論新社) * 地中に眠る古代の[[播磨]](1999年12月、[[神戸新聞]]) * [[海人]]たちの世界―東海の海の役割 The14th Symposium of Kasugai （2008年5月、[[中日出版社]])
== 参考文献 == *{{Cite|和書|title=同志社校友会名簿 昭和36年8月31日現在|editor=同志社校友会|publisher=同志社校友会|year=1961|ref=harv}}{{全国書誌番号|62003710}} == 脚注 == {{reflist}}

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html Mori Kōichi Library] - Kasugai City

[[Category:Japanese archaeologists]] [[Category:Academic staff of Doshisha University]] [[Category:People from Osaka]] [[Category:1928 births]] [[Category:2013 deaths]] [[category:new pages]][[ja:森浩一]]

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html Mori Kōichi Library] - Kasugai City

He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>
== References ==

== External Links == * [https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html Mori Kōichi Library] - Kasugai City

== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{reflist}}

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | Image = | Image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | image = | image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | image = | image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013年8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.

He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|12=draft}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|12=draft}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群|12=draft}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|12=draft}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=draft}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=draft}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.

Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市|12=draft}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=draft}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=draft}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | image = | image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshisha University|qq=}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka City|ja|大阪市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka City|qq=}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Prefecture|qq=}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Group|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mozu Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Mozu Tombs|qq=}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|qq=}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshisha University|qq=}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Shinzaike Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzaike Kofun Group|qq=}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|ja|同志社大学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshisha University|qq=}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|qq=}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai City|ja|春日井市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugai City|qq=}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|qq=}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|qq=}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]].
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|qq=}} and later completed a degree in English literature at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzaike Kofun Group]]. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|qq=}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|qq=}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|qq=}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>
