revision.text:
{{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | image = | image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]] | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | Workplaces = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]].
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|qq=}} and later completed a degree in English literature at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzaike Kofun Group]]. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|qq=}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{reflist}}

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]].
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|qq=|1=Mikunigaoka High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11440990}} and later completed a degree in English literature at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzaike Kofun Group]]. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisen Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|qq=|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzaike Kofun Group]]. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]].
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|1=Mikunigaoka High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11440990}} and later completed a degree in English literature at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzaike Kofun Group]]. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisen Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]].
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|1=Mikunigaoka High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11440990}} and later completed a degree in English literature at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzaike Kofun Group]]. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisen Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]]. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]].
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|1=Mikunigaoka High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11440990}} and later completed a degree in English literature at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzaike Kofun Group]]. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisen Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to [[:en:Kasugai, Aichi|Kasugai City]], establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]]|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai, Aichi|en|Kasugai, Aichi|WD=Q26101|lt=Kasugai City}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}}|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كويتشي موري]][[arz:كويتشى مورى]][[ja:森浩一]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كويتشي موري]][[arz:كويتشى مورى]][[ja:森浩一]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كويتشي موري]][[arz:كويتشى مورى]][[ja:森浩一]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1928|7|17}}|death_date={{death date|2013|8|6}}|birth_place={{ill|Osaka|WD=Q35765}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|archaeologist|WD=Q3621491}}|native_name=森浩一|given_name={{ill|Kōichi|WD=Q11752203}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11539977]] Wikidata name: Kōichi Mori [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Person | Name = Mori Kōichi | image = | image size = | Caption = | Birth date = {{birth date|1928|7|17}} | Birth place = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City}} | Death date = {{death date and age|2013|8|6|1928|7|17}} | Death place = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City}} | Fields = Japanese Archaeology | Alma mater = {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} | Workplaces = {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} }}
'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|1=Mikunigaoka High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11440990}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11502665|lt=Shinzaike Kofun Group}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisen Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai, Aichi|en|Kasugai, Aichi|WD=Q26101|lt=Kasugai City}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}}|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

{{reflist}}

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|1=Mikunigaoka High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11440990}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11502665|lt=Shinzaike Kofun Group}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisen Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai, Aichi|en|Kasugai, Aichi|WD=Q26101|lt=Kasugai City}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Minakata Kumagusu Prize|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}}|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html Mori Kōichi Library] - Kasugai City

He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai, Aichi|en|Kasugai, Aichi|WD=Q26101|lt=Kasugai City|1=Kasugai, Aichi}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Q11407876|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}}|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>

'''Mori Kōichi''' (森 浩一, July 17, 1928 – August 6, 2013)<ref name="nikkei20130809">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASHC0902Y_Z00C13A8AC8000/ 森浩一氏が死去 同志社大名誉教授、古代史ブームけん引] 日本経済新聞 2013rd year 8月9日閲覧</ref> was a Japanese archaeologist and professor emeritus at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|qid=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}}. His work focused on Japanese archaeology and cultural history.
== Biography ==
Mori was born in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka City|1=Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, and spent his childhood in nearby Sakai. His interest in {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} and imperial tombs began with exposure to the myths in government-issued textbooks and the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|qid=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs|1=Mozu Tombs}}.
He graduated from {{ill|Mikunikoka High School|ja|大阪府立三国丘高等学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikunikoka High School|1=Mikunigaoka High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11440990}} and later completed a degree in English literature at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|qid=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}} in 1951.<ref name="kouyuukai">{{harvnb|同志社校友会|1961}}</ref> Mori also earned a master's degree in cultural history in 1957. During his studies, he participated in excavations and co-founded the "Ancient Studies Research Society."
After graduation, Mori worked as a high school teacher while continuing archaeological excavations, including work on the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11502665|lt=Shinzaike Kofun Group|1=Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster}}. In 1965, Mori joined the faculty at {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|qid=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}} and became a full professor in 1972. He retired in 1999.
== Academic Contributions ==
Mori was a prominent figure in Japanese archaeology, engaging in debates and research that challenged mainstream theories. He proposed a domestic origin for the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}} and prioritized a regional approach to archaeology.
Mori also advocated renaming imperial tombs based on their local names, such as calling the {{ill|Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja|仁徳天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku-ryō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5849431}} the "Daisen Kofun." This perspective has influenced modern educational materials.
== Selected Works ==
Mori published extensively, including more than 100 general books and numerous academic papers. Notable works include: * ''The Archaeology of Ancient Japan'' (1993) * ''Revisiting the Records of the Wa People'' (2010)
== Legacy ==
Mori's library was donated to {{ill|Kasugai, Aichi|en|Kasugai, Aichi|qid=Q26101|lt=Kasugai City|1=Kasugai, Aichi}}, establishing the {{ill|Mori Kōichi Library|ja|森浩一文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Kōichi Library|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 2010.<ref>[https://www.city.kasugai.lg.jp/shimin/bunka/bunkazai/midokoro/moribunko.html 森浩一文庫]</ref> He was awarded the {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu Prize|ja|南方熊楠賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakata Kumagusu Prize|1=Q11407876|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11407876}} in 2012 for his lifelong contributions to archaeology and ancient history.<ref name=朝日>{{cite news|url= http://www.asahi.com/eco/news/OSK201203080018.html|title=南方熊楠賞に考古学者・森浩一さん|publisher={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|qid=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}}|accessdate=2020-7-26|date=2012-3-8}}</ref>
